cause: Staphylococcus aureus or Streptococcus pyogenes
epidemiology: Common in children in tropical and temperate regions, especially in low-resource settings with poor hygiene.
treatment: Clean with antiseptic. Topical mupirocin 2% TID for 5-7 days. If extensive, oral cephalexin 25 mg/kg BID for 7 days.
referral_criteria: No improvement after 48 hours of treatment, signs of cellulitis, or recurrent episodes.
prevention: Improve hygiene, wash with soap and water, trim nails, avoid sharing towels.
who_guideline: WHO essential medicines list: mupirocin or fusidic acid for localized impetigo.

cause: Streptococcus pyogenes (group A strep), Staphylococcus aureus
epidemiology: Occurs worldwide, risk increases with breaks in skin (wounds, ulcers, fungal infections). Common in diabetic patients.
treatment: Oral antibiotics: cephalexin 500 mg QID or cloxacillin 500 mg QID for 7-10 days. Severe cases require IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone).
referral_criteria: Signs of systemic infection (fever, chills, tachycardia), rapid spread, facial involvement, immunocompromised patient.
prevention: Treat predisposing conditions (tinea pedis, wounds), moisturize dry skin to prevent cracking.
who_guideline: WHO IMCI guidelines recommend referral for any red flag signs in children.

cause: Dermatophyte fungi (Trichophyton rubrum, Microsporum canis)
epidemiology: Common in warm, humid tropical climates. Affects all ages. Spread by direct contact or fomites.
treatment: Topical terbinafine 1% once daily for 2 weeks OR clotrimazole 1% BID for 4 weeks. Widespread cases: oral terbinafine 250 mg daily for 2-4 weeks.
referral_criteria: Extensive or resistant cases, immunosuppressed patients, uncertain diagnosis.
prevention: Keep skin dry, avoid sharing clothing/towels, treat affected household members.
who_guideline: WHO recommends topical azoles or terbinafine as first-line treatment.

cause: Varicella-zoster virus reactivation
epidemiology: Risk increases with age (>50 years) and immunosuppression. More common in tropics where childhood varicella is universal.
treatment: Oral acyclovir 800 mg 5x daily for 7 days (start within 72 hours). Pain management: paracetamol, NSAIDs, or gabapentin for post-herpetic neuralgia.
referral_criteria: Ophthalmic involvement (eye), disseminated rash, immunocompromised patient, severe pain.
prevention: Vaccination (live attenuated zoster vaccine recommended for adults >50 years).
who_guideline: WHO recommends acyclovir for treatment, especially in immunocompromised and elderly patients.

cause: Leishmania tropica, L. major, L. braziliensis (protozoan parasite transmitted by sandflies)
epidemiology: Endemic in tropical and subtropical regions: Africa, Middle East, Central/South America, Indian subcontinent. Rural areas.
treatment: Intralesional sodium stibogluconate or topical paromomycin 15%/gentamicin 0.5% BID for 20 days. Systemic therapy for complex cases.
referral_criteria: Multiple lesions (>5), large lesions (>5 cm), mucosal involvement, treatment failure.
prevention: Insecticide-treated bed nets, repellents, sandfly control programs.
who_guideline: WHO recommends intralesional antimonials or topical paromomycin as first-line for uncomplicated cutaneous leishmaniasis.

cause: Treponema pallidum pertenue (spirochete)
epidemiology: Endemic in tropical Africa, Southeast Asia, and Pacific Islands. Affects children 2-15 years in poor rural communities.
treatment: Single dose azithromycin 30 mg/kg oral (max 2 g) or benzathine penicillin 1.2 million units IM.
referral_criteria: Suspected tertiary yaws (bone/joint involvement), treatment failure after 4 weeks.
prevention: WHO yaws eradication campaign: mass drug administration with azithromycin in endemic areas.
who_guideline: WHO recommends single-dose azithromycin as first-line treatment for yaws eradication.

cause: Mycobacterium ulcerans (produces mycolactone toxin)
epidemiology: West Africa (especially Côte d'Ivoire, Ghana, Benin), also parts of Asia and Australia. Slow-growing, painless ulcer.
treatment: Rifampicin 10 mg/kg daily + clarithromycin 7.5 mg/kg BID or moxifloxacin 400 mg daily for 8 weeks.
referral_criteria: All confirmed cases should be referred for treatment. WHO recommends referral to specialized Buruli ulcer treatment centers.
prevention: Wear protective clothing in endemic areas, avoid contact with contaminated water sources.
who_guideline: WHO recommends 8-week antibiotic therapy (rifampicin + clarithromycin) followed by surgery if needed.

cause: Sarcoptes scabiei var. hominis (mite)
epidemiology: Common in overcrowded conditions worldwide, especially tropical regions. High prevalence in children and institutional settings.
treatment: Permethrin 5% cream applied head-to-toe overnight, repeated after 7 days. Oral ivermectin 200 mcg/kg single dose (repeat after 7 days).
referral_criteria: Crusted (Norwegian) scabies, secondary infection with cellulitis, treatment failure.
prevention: Treat all household contacts simultaneously, wash bedding in hot water, avoid close contact until treated.
who_guideline: WHO NTD roadmap includes scabies control. Ivermectin mass drug administration in endemic communities.

cause: Fungal (eumycetoma) or bacterial (actinomycetoma) organisms
epidemiology: Endemic in tropical Africa, India, and Central America. Between 15°S and 30°N. Most common in barefoot agricultural workers.
treatment: Eumycetoma: Itraconazole 400 mg daily for 12+ months + surgery. Actinomycetoma: Streptomycin 14 mg/kg IM daily + dapsone 1.5 mg/kg BID for months.
referral_criteria: All suspected mycetoma should be referred. Requires specialized care for prolonged treatment.
prevention: Wear shoes/protective footwear when working outdoors in endemic areas.
who_guideline: WHO recognizes mycetoma as a neglected tropical disease. Long-term antifungal therapy essential for eumycetoma.

cause: Burkholderia pseudomallei (gram-negative bacillus)
epidemiology: Endemic in Southeast Asia (especially Thailand, Vietnam) and northern Australia. Associated with rice paddies and wet soil.
treatment: Intensive phase: IV ceftazidime 50 mg/kg QID or meropenem 25 mg/kg TID for 14+ days. Eradication: TMP-SMX 8/40 mg/kg BID for 12-20 weeks.
referral_criteria: All suspected cases require immediate referral for IV antibiotics. High mortality if untreated.
prevention: Avoid contact with soil and water in endemic areas, wear protective footwear, treat wounds promptly.
who_guideline: WHO recommends hospitalization for intensive IV antibiotic therapy followed by prolonged oral eradication.

cause: Mycobacterium leprae / M. lepromatosis
epidemiology: Endemic in India, Brazil, Indonesia, and parts of Africa. Transmission via respiratory droplets. Long incubation period (2-10 years).
treatment: Paucibacillary: Rifampicin 600 mg monthly + dapsone 100 mg daily for 6 months. Multibacillary: Add clofazimine 300 mg monthly, 50 mg daily for 12 months.
referral_criteria: All confirmed or suspected cases should be referred to a leprosy control program for treatment and monitoring.
prevention: BCG vaccination provides some protection. Contact screening in endemic areas. WHO provides free MDT.
who_guideline: WHO-recommended multidrug therapy (MDT) is the standard of care, provided free through WHO.

cause: Dengue virus (DENV-1-4, flavivirus transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes)
epidemiology: Endemic in >100 tropical countries. Major cause of fever with rash in Southeast Asia, Latin America, and Africa.
treatment: Supportive care: paracetamol for fever (avoid NSAIDs/aspirin due to bleeding risk). Monitor for warning signs (abdominal pain, vomiting, mucosal bleeding).
referral_criteria: Any warning sign, rising hematocrit with rapid platelet drop, severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, lethargy.
prevention: Mosquito control (eliminate breeding sites), insect repellent, bed nets. Dengue vaccine (CYD-TDV) available for seropositive individuals.
who_guideline: WHO dengue classification: dengue without warning signs, dengue with warning signs, severe dengue.

cause: Chikungunya virus (alphavirus, transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes)
epidemiology: Epidemics in Africa, Asia, Indian subcontinent, and the Americas. Often co-circulates with dengue.
treatment: Supportive care: paracetamol, NSAIDs for joint pain (after excluding dengue). Rest and physical therapy for chronic joint symptoms.
referral_criteria: Severe joint pain limiting mobility, persistent symptoms beyond 3 weeks, neurological signs.
prevention: Same as dengue: mosquito avoidance, repellents, bed nets. No specific vaccine widely available.
who_guideline: WHO emphasizes symptomatic treatment. No specific antiviral treatment available.

cause: Measles virus (paramyxovirus)
epidemiology: Outbreaks in unvaccinated populations worldwide. High mortality in malnourished children in developing countries.
treatment: Supportive care: vitamin A 200,000 IU daily for 2 days (reduces mortality). Treat secondary infections with appropriate antibiotics.
referral_criteria: Severe pneumonia, encephalitis, severe dehydration, malnutrition, immunocompromised patient.
prevention: MMR vaccine (two doses). WHO recommends >95% vaccination coverage for herd immunity.
who_guideline: WHO recommends vitamin A for all children with measles in settings where deficiency is common.

cause: Multifactorial: genetic (filaggrin mutation), environmental, immune dysregulation
epidemiology: Common worldwide, increasing in developing countries. Onset usually in early childhood.
treatment: Emollients regularly. Topical hydrocortisone 1% for mild, topical tacrolimus for face. Treat secondary infection with antibiotics.
referral_criteria: Severe refractory disease, suspected eczema herpeticum, significant impact on quality of life.
prevention: Regular moisturizing, avoid triggers (soaps, allergens), wear soft cotton clothing.
who_guideline: WHO essential medicines list includes topical corticosteroids for eczema treatment.

cause: Autoimmune (T-cell mediated), genetic predisposition (HLA-Cw6), triggered by infections, stress, medications
epidemiology: Prevalence 0.5-3% worldwide, lower in tropical regions. Peaks at ages 20-30 and 50-60.
treatment: Topical: vitamin D analogs, corticosteroids, coal tar. Systemic for severe: methotrexate, phototherapy, biologics.
referral_criteria: Severe/extensive disease, psoriatic arthritis, treatment failure with topical therapy.
prevention: Avoid triggers: stress, beta-blockers, lithium, alcohol. Maintain skin hydration.
who_guideline: WHO Global Report on Psoriasis highlights need for access to treatment in low-resource settings.

cause: Mast cell degranulation from allergens, infections, medications, physical triggers (heat, cold, pressure)
epidemiology: Affects 15-25% of population at least once. Acute (<6 weeks) vs chronic (>6 weeks).
treatment: Antihistamines (cetirizine 10 mg, loratadine 10 mg). Severe: short course oral prednisolone 30-40 mg daily for 3-5 days.
referral_criteria: Chronic urticaria (>6 weeks), angioedema without wheals, suspected autoimmune cause.
prevention: Avoid known triggers, keep a diary to identify triggers.
who_guideline: WHO model formulary includes oral antihistamines as first-line treatment.

cause: Dermatophytes: Trichophyton, Microsporum, Epidermophyton
epidemiology: Very common in tropical climates due to heat and humidity. Affects all ages.
treatment: Topical terbinafine 1% or clotrimazole 1% BID for 2-4 weeks. Oral terbinafine for scalp/nail involvement.
referral_criteria: Extensive involvement, scalp infection (tinea capitis), nail infection, immunocompromised patient.
prevention: Keep skin dry, wear breathable fabrics, avoid sharing towels, treat athlete's foot promptly.
who_guideline: WHO Model List of Essential Medicines includes clotrimazole and terbinafine.

cause: Irritant (chemical, soap) or allergic (nickel, poison ivy, latex) reaction
epidemiology: Common worldwide, accounts for 4-7% of dermatology visits. Occupational dermatitis common in tropical agriculture.
treatment: Remove offending agent. Topical corticosteroids (hydrocortisone 1% for mild, betamethasone for severe). Antihistamines for itching.
referral_criteria: Severe or widespread reaction, suspected occupational cause requiring patch testing, secondary infection.
prevention: Identify and avoid triggers, use barrier creams/protective gloves for occupational exposure.
who_guideline: WHO essential medicines: hydrocortisone cream for inflammatory skin conditions.

cause: Varicella-zoster virus (primary infection)
epidemiology: Universal childhood infection in unvaccinated populations. More severe in adults and immunocompromised.
treatment: Supportive care: calamine lotion, antihistamines, paracetamol for fever. Acyclovir for severe cases or at-risk patients.
referral_criteria: Immunocompromised patient, severe disease, pneumonitis, encephalitis, secondary bacterial infection.
prevention: Varicella vaccine (routine childhood immunization).
who_guideline: WHO recommends varicella vaccination for routine childhood immunization programs.

cause: Streptococcus pyogenes (group A strep) producing erythrogenic toxin
epidemiology: Primarily affects children 5-15 years. Outbreaks in schools and communities worldwide.
treatment: Penicillin V 250 mg BID for 10 days or amoxicillin 50 mg/kg daily for 10 days. Treat household contacts if symptomatic.
referral_criteria: Signs of complicated disease: rheumatic fever, glomerulonephritis, peritonsillar abscess.
prevention: Good hygiene, cover mouth when coughing. No vaccine available.
who_guideline: WHO recommends penicillin as first-line treatment for group A streptococcal infections.

cause: Molluscum contagiosum virus (MCV, poxvirus)
epidemiology: Common in children worldwide. Spread by direct contact and fomites. More prevalent in tropical climates.
treatment: Usually self-resolving in 6-12 months. Options: cryotherapy, curettage, cantharidin, topical imiquimod.
referral_criteria: Immunocompromised patient (HIV), extensive disease, uncertain diagnosis.
prevention: Avoid direct contact with lesions, do not share towels, good hand hygiene.
who_guideline: WHO notes it is common in HIV-positive patients and can be more extensive.

cause: Wuchereria bancrofti, Brugia malayi (filarial nematodes transmitted by mosquitoes)
epidemiology: Endemic in tropical Africa, Asia, Western Pacific, and parts of Latin America. Leading cause of permanent disability worldwide.
treatment: Annual mass drug administration: ivermectin + albendazole (plus DEC in non-onchocerciasis areas). Acute attacks: antibiotics for secondary infection.
referral_criteria: Acute adenolymphangitis, secondary cellulitis, hydrocele requiring surgery.
prevention: Mosquito control, insecticide-treated bed nets, annual MDA in endemic areas.
who_guideline: WHO Global Programme to Eliminate Lymphatic Filariasis uses annual MDA as primary strategy.

cause: Bacterial (Staphylococcus aureus, Haemophilus influenzae) or viral (adenovirus)
epidemiology: Extremely common worldwide. Highly contagious. Outbreaks in schools and communities. Leading cause of eye clinic visits in LMICs.
treatment: Bacterial: topical antibiotic drops (chloramphenicol 0.5% QID for 7 days or tetracycline 1% ointment). Viral: supportive care, cold compresses, artificial tears.
referral_criteria: No improvement after 48 hours of antibiotic treatment, severe pain, photophobia, vision changes, neonatal conjunctivitis.
prevention: Hand hygiene, avoid touching/rubbing eyes, do not share towels or eye drops.
who_guideline: WHO recommends chloramphenicol or tetracycline eye drops/ointment for bacterial conjunctivitis.

cause: Chlamydia trachomatis (obligate intracellular bacterium)
epidemiology: Leading infectious cause of blindness worldwide. Endemic in >40 countries, especially sub-Saharan Africa, Middle East, Asia. Affects poorest communities with limited water/sanitation.
treatment: WHO SAFE strategy: Surgery for trichiasis, Azithromycin 20 mg/kg single dose (or tetracycline 1% BID for 6 weeks), Facial cleanliness, Environmental improvement.
referral_criteria: Trichiasis (eyelashes rubbing cornea), corneal opacity, visual impairment. All active cases should be referred to trachoma control program.
prevention: Facial cleanliness, improved sanitation, fly control, mass drug administration with azithromycin in endemic areas.
who_guideline: WHO Alliance for Global Elimination of Trachoma by 2020 (GET2020) uses SAFE strategy.

cause: Candida albicans (fungus)
epidemiology: Common in infants, elderly, HIV/AIDS patients, after antibiotic use, and in diabetics. Marker of immunosuppression in adults.
treatment: Topical: nystatin suspension 100,000 units QID for 7-14 days, or clotrimazole troches 5x daily. Systemic: fluconazole 200 mg oral once then 100 mg daily for 2 weeks.
referral_criteria: HIV suspected (test and refer), immunocompromised patient, failure of topical therapy, difficulty swallowing (suspect esophageal candidiasis).
prevention: Good oral hygiene, rinse mouth after steroid inhaler use, treat underlying immunosuppression.
who_guideline: WHO recommends fluconazole as first-line treatment for oral candidiasis in HIV patients.

cause: Mixed bacteria: Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus pyogenes, gram-negatives
epidemiology: Common in all settings, especially agricultural work in tropics where wounds are exposed to soil. High risk in diabetics and immunocompromised.
treatment: Clean with sterile saline, remove necrotic tissue, topical antiseptic. Oral antibiotics: cephalexin 500 mg QID or co-amoxiclav 625 mg TID for 7 days.
referral_criteria: Signs of systemic infection (fever, chills), rapidly spreading redness, necrotizing fasciitis suspected, immunocompromised patient, foreign body.
prevention: Clean wounds promptly, keep covered, sterile dressing technique, tetanus vaccination.
who_guideline: WHO surgical site infection guidelines recommend antibiotic prophylaxis for contaminated wounds.

cause: Thermal: flame, hot liquid (scald), hot object, electricity, chemicals
epidemiology: Major cause of morbidity in LMICs. Children most affected. Cooking accidents, kerosene lamps, and open fires common causes.
treatment: Superficial (1st degree): cool water, aloe vera, pain relief. Partial-thickness (2nd): clean, topical silver sulfadiazine 1%, sterile dressing. Full-thickness (3rd): surgical management.
referral_criteria: Full-thickness burn, >10% body surface area, facial/airway burn, circumferential burn of chest/limb, child or elderly, electrical/chemical burn.
prevention: Keep children away from cooking fires, use safe stoves, install smoke detectors, first aid education.
who_guideline: WHO Emergency Triage Assessment and Treatment (ETAT) guidelines recommend immediate referral for severe burns.

cause: Venomous snake bite (viper, elapid, sea snake)
epidemiology: Major public health problem in tropical Africa, Asia, and Latin America. 5 million bites annually, 100,000 deaths. Agricultural workers and children at highest risk.
treatment: Pressure immobilization bandage, keep patient calm and still. Antivenom (specific to regional snakes) IV as soon as indicated. Tetanus prophylaxis. Avoid tourniquets, cutting, or suction.
referral_criteria: ALL snake bites with signs of envenomation require immediate referral. Signs: local swelling beyond bite site, bleeding, ptosis, respiratory difficulty, neurotoxicity.
prevention: Wear boots and long pants in snake-prone areas, use a light at night, do not disturb snakes, clear vegetation around homes.
who_guideline: WHO categorizes snakebite as a neglected tropical disease. Recommends region-specific antivenom.

cause: Dog bite (mechanical trauma + potential rabies virus transmission)
epidemiology: Worldwide, >99% of rabies deaths from dog bites. Highest burden in Africa and Asia. Children 5-14 years most affected.
treatment: Wound cleaning with soap and water for 15 min. Rabies post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP): rabies vaccine 4 doses (days 0, 3, 7, 14). Rabies immunoglobulin for Category III bites. Tetanus prophylaxis. Antibiotics for infected bites.
referral_criteria: ALL bites with potential rabies exposure require immediate referral for PEP. Signs of infection, suspected rabid animal, bites on head/neck/hands.
prevention: Dog vaccination programs, stray dog control, avoid approaching unfamiliar dogs, educate children on dog safety.
who_guideline: WHO rabies guidelines: immediate wound care + PEP for all Category II and III bites. Rabies vaccines and immunoglobulin recommended.

cause: Bacillus anthracis (gram-positive, spore-forming rod)
epidemiology: Endemic in agricultural regions of Africa, Asia, and Central America. Acquired from contact with infected animals or animal products (hides, wool, bone meal).
treatment: Ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID or doxycycline 100 mg BID for 7-10 days. Severe cases require IV antibiotics (ciprofloxacin + clindamycin).
referral_criteria: All suspected cases require immediate referral. Signs of systemic disease: fever, sepsis, respiratory involvement, meningeal signs.
prevention: Vaccinate livestock in endemic areas. Avoid contact with sick/dead animals. Safe disposal of animal carcasses. Occupational hygiene for leather/wool workers.
who_guideline: WHO classifies anthrax as a zoonotic disease requiring immediate notification. Antibiotic treatment plus possible antitoxin.

cause: Onchocerca volvulus (filarial nematode transmitted by black flies Simulium)
epidemiology: Endemic in sub-Saharan Africa (99%), also Yemen and Americas. Near fast-flowing rivers where black flies breed. Leading infectious cause of blindness after trachoma.
treatment: Ivermectin 150-200 mcg/kg single dose every 6-12 months. Annual mass drug administration in endemic areas. Treat for 10-15 years of adult worm lifespan.
referral_criteria: Suspected onchocerciasis, severe skin disease, visual impairment, nodules in head/neck region (higher risk of eye involvement).
prevention: Mass drug administration with ivermectin (Mectizan donation program). Vector control with larviciding. Personal protection with repellents.
who_guideline: WHO NTD roadmap targets elimination of onchocerciasis through MDA with ivermectin.

cause: Loa loa (filarial nematode transmitted by Chrysops deer flies)
epidemiology: Endemic in Central and West Africa (Cameroon, DRC, Congo, Nigeria, Gabon). Risk to travelers and expatriates. Highest burden in rainforest areas.
treatment: Diethylcarbamazine (DEC) 6 mg/kg daily for 21 days (start low, escalate dose). Antihistamines for allergic reactions. Surgical removal of adult worm from eye.
referral_criteria: High microfilarial load (risk of encephalopathy with DEC), eye worm, neurological symptoms, renal involvement.
prevention: Personal protection (repellents, protective clothing), vector control. No effective preventive chemotherapy exists.
who_guideline: WHO notes Loa loa endemicity affects MDA strategy for onchocerciasis and lymphatic filariasis due to risk of severe adverse events.

cause: Tunga penetrans (sand flea, jigger flea) - female flea burrows into skin
epidemiology: Endemic in sub-Saharan Africa, Latin America, and Caribbean. Affects poorest communities with sandy soil. Children 5-14 years and elderly most affected.
treatment: Surgical extraction of intact flea using sterile needle/curette. Topical: dimethicone 4% lotion, or ivermectin 200 mcg/kg single dose. Tetanus prophylaxis. Manage secondary infection.
referral_criteria: Multiple lesions (>20), severe secondary infection with cellulitis, gangrene, tetanus risk, immobility.
prevention: Wear closed shoes, avoid walking barefoot in sandy areas, insect repellent, treat floors with insecticide in infested homes.
who_guideline: WHO recognizes tungiasis as a neglected tropical disease. Prevention through footwear and environmental hygiene.

cause: Ancylostoma braziliense, A. caninum (hookworms of dogs/cats) - larvae migrate in skin
epidemiology: Common in tropical and subtropical regions. Associated with beaches, playgrounds, and areas contaminated with dog/cat feces. Affects travelers and locals.
treatment: Oral albendazole 400 mg daily for 3-7 days OR ivermectin 200 mcg/kg single dose (often curative). Topical: thiabendazole 10% cream for localized lesions.
referral_criteria: Extensive or bullous lesions, secondary infection, treatment failure, immunocompromised patient.
prevention: Avoid walking barefoot on beaches/soil in endemic areas, deworm pets, fence playgrounds from animal feces.
who_guideline: WHO recommends albendazole or ivermectin for treatment of cutaneous larva migrans.

cause: Malassezia globosa, M. furfur (lipophilic yeast)
epidemiology: Common worldwide, more prevalent in tropical climates due to heat and humidity. Affects adolescents and young adults most frequently.
treatment: Topical: ketoconazole 2% shampoo (apply 5 min, rinse) or clotrimazole 1% BID for 2-4 weeks. Resistant: oral itraconazole 200 mg daily for 7 days.
referral_criteria: Extensive involvement unresponsive to treatment, uncertain diagnosis, immunocompromised patient.
prevention: Keep skin dry, avoid excessive sweating, use antifungal shampoo periodically if recurrent.
who_guideline: WHO essential medicines list includes ketoconazole and itraconazole for fungal infections.

cause: Malassezia furfur, immune response, genetic factors, exacerbated by stress and cold weather
epidemiology: Affects 3-5% of adults worldwide. Bimodal: peaks in infancy (cradle cap) and adults 30-60 years. Common in HIV patients (often severe).
treatment: Scalp: ketoconazole 2% shampoo or zinc pyrithione shampoo twice weekly. Face: topical hydrocortisone 1% short term, ketoconazole cream. Severe: oral itraconazole.
referral_criteria: Severe refractory disease, HIV suspected, widespread involvement, secondary infection.
prevention: Regular use of medicated shampoos, avoid triggers (stress, cold), manage underlying HIV if present.
who_guideline: WHO essential medicines: topical corticosteroids and antifungals for seborrheic dermatitis.

cause: Dermatophytes: Microsporum canis, Trichophyton tonsurans, T. violaceum
epidemiology: Common in children worldwide. High prevalence in Africa and Asia. Spread via shared combs, hats, and pillows. More common in crowded conditions.
treatment: Oral terbinafine: children <20 kg: 62.5 mg daily, 20-40 kg: 125 mg daily, >40 kg: 250 mg daily for 4-6 weeks. Topical alone is insufficient. Add ketoconazole shampoo to reduce transmission.
referral_criteria: Kerion (inflamed, boggy mass), secondary bacterial infection, treatment failure after 8 weeks, uncertain diagnosis.
prevention: Avoid sharing combs/brushes/hats, treat infected family members, treat pets if source.
who_guideline: WHO essential medicines list includes terbinafine for dermatophyte infections.

cause: Multifactorial: sebum overproduction, follicular hyperkeratinization, Cutibacterium acnes, inflammation, hormonal factors
epidemiology: Affects 85% of adolescents and young adults worldwide. Can persist into adulthood. Prevalence increasing in LMICs with Western diet.
treatment: Mild: topical benzoyl peroxide 2.5-5% or adapalene 0.1% gel. Moderate: add topical clindamycin 1% or oral doxycycline 100 mg BID for 8-12 weeks. Severe: oral isotretinoin (requires specialist).
referral_criteria: Severe nodulocystic acne, scarring, treatment failure, psychological distress, isotretinoin consideration (requires monitoring).
prevention: Gentle cleansing, avoid picking/popting lesions, non-comedogenic products, avoid triggers (stress, dairy in some).
who_guideline: WHO essential medicines list includes benzoyl peroxide, topical antibiotics, and oral tetracyclines for acne.

cause: Staphylococcus aureus (producing exfoliative toxins A/B)
epidemiology: More common in neonates and young children. Outbreaks in nurseries, daycares. Can be confused with burns or scalds.
treatment: Gentle cleansing, topical mupirocin 2% TID. Extensive: oral cephalexin 25 mg/kg BID or cloxacillin 12.5 mg/kg QID for 7 days.
referral_criteria: Widespread bullae, neonate, signs of staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome (SSSS), immunocompromised.
prevention: Good hygiene, avoid sharing towels/bedding, keep lesions covered, hand washing.
who_guideline: WHO recommends topical antibiotics for localized impetigo and systemic antibiotics for extensive cases.

cause: Streptococcus pyogenes (group A strep) — distinct from cellulitis by well-demarcated border
epidemiology: Common in all regions. Predisposing factors: breaks in skin, fungal infections (tinea pedis), lymphedema, venous insufficiency. Facial erysipelas more common in elderly.
treatment: Oral penicillin V 500 mg QID or amoxicillin 500 mg TID for 7-10 days. Severe: IV benzylpenicillin or ceftriaxone.
referral_criteria: Systemic toxicity, rapid progression, eyelid involvement (risk of cavernous sinus thrombosis), immunocompromised, treatment failure.
prevention: Treat predisposing tinea pedis, good skin hygiene, moisturize dry skin to prevent cracking.
who_guideline: WHO guidelines: penicillin or amoxicillin as first-line treatment for erysipelas.

cause: Human papillomavirus (HPV) types 1, 2, 4, 27, 57
epidemiology: Common worldwide. Peak in children and adolescents. Spread by direct contact and autoinoculation. Higher prevalence in immunocompromised.
treatment: Topical: salicylic acid 40% daily, or cryotherapy with liquid nitrogen every 2-4 weeks. Persistent: imiquimod 5% or intralesional candida antigen.
referral_criteria: Extensive or resistant warts, immunocompromised patient, genital warts (refer for STI screening), atypical appearance.
prevention: Avoid picking warts, don't share towels/nail files, use flip-flops in public showers, HPV vaccine prevents some types.
who_guideline: WHO recommends cryotherapy or salicylic acid for common and plantar warts.

cause: Coxsackievirus A16, Enterovirus 71, other enteroviruses
epidemiology: Common in children <10 years. Outbreaks in daycares/schools. Peak in tropical regions: rainy season. Enterovirus 71 strains can cause neurological complications.
treatment: Supportive care: paracetamol for fever, cool foods/liquids, mouth rinses. No specific antiviral. Monitor for neurological signs.
referral_criteria: High fever >3 days, lethargy, neck stiffness, seizures, rapid breathing, dehydration from poor intake.
prevention: Hand hygiene, disinfect contaminated surfaces, avoid close contact with infected children. No vaccine widely available.
who_guideline: WHO recommends supportive treatment and monitoring for complications, especially with EV71 strains.

cause: Multiple STIs: syphilis (Treponema pallidum), herpes (HSV-2), chancroid (H. ducreyi), LGV (C. trachomatis L1-L3)
epidemiology: Major public health problem in sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia. Increases HIV transmission risk 2-5x. Common in sex workers and their clients.
treatment: Syphilis: benzathine penicillin 2.4 million units IM single dose. Herpes: acyclovir 400 mg TID for 7-10 days. Chancroid: azithromycin 1 g single dose. LGV: doxycycline 100 mg BID for 21 days.
referral_criteria: All genital ulcers should be referred for STI screening, HIV testing, and syndromic management. Treatment failure, suspected syphilis in pregnancy.
prevention: Condom use, partner notification and treatment, HIV testing, circumcision reduces HIV/STI risk.
who_guideline: WHO syndromic management approach for genital ulcer disease. All patients should have HIV testing.

cause: Staphylococcus aureus, E. coli, Group B Streptococcus, other bacteria colonizing umbilical stump
epidemiology: Common in home births in LMICs with poor cord care. Risk factor for neonatal sepsis. Leading cause of neonatal mortality in some regions.
treatment: Mild: topical antiseptic (chlorhexidine 4%) TID. Moderate to severe: oral or IV antibiotics (cloxacillin + gentamicin). Tetanus prophylaxis if not vaccinated.
referral_criteria: ALL neonatal omphalitis beyond mild redness requires immediate referral. Signs of sepsis: fever, poor feeding, lethargy, rapid breathing.
prevention: Clean cord care: chlorhexidine 4% applied to cord stump daily, dry cord care, clean delivery practices, hand washing.
who_guideline: WHO recommends chlorhexidine 4% for umbilical cord care in settings with high neonatal mortality.

cause: Chemical (silver nitrate prophylaxis), bacterial (Chlamydia trachomatis, Neisseria gonorrhoeae), or viral
epidemiology: Prevalent in regions with high maternal STI rates. Neisseria gonorrhoeae can cause rapid corneal perforation and blindness. Chlamydia causes chronic infection.
treatment: Gentle saline irrigation. Gonococcal: IV/IM ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg single dose + frequent saline washes. Chlamydial: oral erythromycin 12.5 mg/kg QID for 14 days. Topical antibiotics alone insufficient.
referral_criteria: ALL neonatal conjunctivitis requires immediate referral. Corneal involvement, purulent discharge with eyelid swelling (presumed gonococcal) is an emergency.
prevention: Screening/treatment of maternal STIs. Silver nitrate 1% or tetracycline 1% ointment at birth. Clean delivery.
who_guideline: WHO recommends Universal eye care at birth with 1% tetracycline ointment or silver nitrate solution.

cause: Polymicrobial (Type I): anaerobes + aerobes. Group A strep (Type II). Rapidly progressive soft tissue infection.
epidemiology: Rare but life-threatening. Risk factors: diabetes, immunosuppression, peripheral vascular disease, recent surgery or trauma. Higher incidence in tropical regions.
treatment: Surgical emergency: immediate aggressive debridement. Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam + clindamycin). ICU support. Early recognition is critical.
referral_criteria: SUSPECTED NECROTIZING FASCIITIS REQUIRES IMMEDIATE SURGICAL REFERRAL. Signs: severe pain out of proportion, rapid progression, crepitus, systemic toxicity.
prevention: Prompt treatment of wounds and skin infections, glycemic control in diabetics, careful surgical technique.
who_guideline: WHO recognizes necrotizing fasciitis as a surgical emergency requiring immediate debridement and broad-spectrum antibiotics.

cause: Fungal (Candida albicans), bacterial (Staphylococcus aureus), nutritional deficiencies (riboflavin, iron, zinc), or mechanical (ill-fitting dentures)
epidemiology: Common in malnutrition, HIV/AIDS, elderly with dentures, and children. High prevalence in food-insecure populations.
treatment: Topical: clotrimazole 1% + hydrocortisone 1% combination cream BID for 7-14 days. Treat underlying nutritional deficiencies. Consider oral vitamin B complex and iron.
referral_criteria: Persistent despite treatment, HIV suspected, severe malnutrition, suspected oral cancer in older adults (if unilateral and non-healing).
prevention: Good nutrition (B vitamins, iron, zinc), treat underlying HIV, proper denture fit, maintain skin moisture at mouth corners.
who_guideline: WHO suggests topical antifungal-steroid combination for angular stomatitis in HIV care guidelines.

cause: Physiological (immature liver), pathological (ABO/Rh incompatibility, G6PD deficiency, sepsis, biliary atresia)
epidemiology: Very common in newborns worldwide. 60% of term and 80% of preterm infants develop jaundice. Severe cases can cause kernicterus (brain damage). Highest risk in sub-Saharan Africa and Asia.
treatment: Physiological: frequent feeding, sunlight exposure (controlled). Phototherapy for moderate levels. Exchange transfusion for severe cases. Phenobarbital for prolonged jaundice.
referral_criteria: Jaundice appearing <24 hours of age, rapidly rising levels, preterm infant, signs of kernicterus (lethargy, poor feeding, high-pitched cry, arching), pale stool (suggests biliary atresia).
prevention: Ensure adequate feeding, monitor for jaundice, early detection using transcutaneous bilirubinometer in high-prevalence settings.
who_guideline: WHO recommends all newborns be monitored for jaundice. Phototherapy is the mainstay of treatment.

cause: Measles virus (paramyxovirus) — prodromal phase before skin rash develops
epidemiology: Outbreaks in unvaccinated populations. Highly contagious (90% attack rate in susceptible contacts). Aerosol transmission.
treatment: Supportive: paracetamol, cough relief, vitamin A 200,000 IU daily for 2 days. Isolate patient. Vaccinate contacts within 72 hours for post-exposure prophylaxis.
referral_criteria: Severe cough/difficulty breathing, inability to drink, convulsions, lethargy, severe malnutrition. All suspected measles should be reported to health authorities.
prevention: MMR vaccine (two doses, first at 9-12 months). >95% coverage needed for herd immunity. Post-exposure vaccination within 72 hours.
who_guideline: WHO measles position paper: vitamin A reduces mortality by 50% in measles-endemic areas.

cause: Dengue virus (secondary infection with different serotype increases risk)
epidemiology: Leading cause of hospitalization in children in Asia and Latin America. Second dengue infection is the main risk factor for severe disease.
treatment: Close monitoring: vital signs, hematocrit, platelet count. IV fluid resuscitation (crystalloids) for plasma leakage. Blood transfusion for severe bleeding. Avoid NSAIDs/aspirin.
referral_criteria: ALL SIGNS OF SEVERE DENGUE REQUIRE IMMEDIATE REFERRAL. Warning signs: severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, mucosal bleeding, lethargy, hepatomegaly, rising hematocrit with rapid platelet drop.
prevention: Same as dengue: mosquito control, insect repellent, bed nets. CYD-TDV vaccine for seropositive individuals only.
who_guideline: WHO classified severe dengue by plasma leakage, severe bleeding, or severe organ involvement.
